No Poverty.reason: The course explicitly covers "Welfare Capitalism" and "welfare States" (Week 14), which directly relate to social protection systems and policies aimed at poverty reduction (Target 1.3).
evidence: The Three Worlds of Welfare Capitalism
The New Politics of the welfare States

Zero Hunger.reason: No explicit or inferable evidence related to hunger, food security, or sustainable agriculture was found in the course syllabus.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course explicitly covers "疫情政治" (Pandemic Politics) in Week 15, directly addressing global health risks (Target 3.d). "Welfare Capitalism" also relates to health systems (Target 3.8).

Quality Education.reason: The course focuses on political systems and research skills, not on the specific targets of quality education such as access, literacy, or educational infrastructure.

Gender Equality.reason: The course covers "Electoral Systems" and "Representation" (Week 5), which can be inferred to include discussions on women's political participation, aligning with Target 5.5.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit or inferable evidence related to water, sanitation, hygiene, or water resource management was found in the course syllabus.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: No explicit or inferable evidence related to energy access, renewable energy, or energy efficiency was found in the course syllabus.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The "Political Economy" section (Weeks 13-14) explicitly discusses "The Developmental State" and "Welfare Capitalism," directly linking to economic growth, productivity, and labor rights (Targets 8.1, 8.2, 8.8).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course covers "The Developmental State" (Week 13), relevant to industrialization (Target 9.2), and "網路 新媒體" (Week 9), which relates to ICT infrastructure (Target 9.c).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course explicitly covers "族群政治" (Ethnic Politics, Week 11) and "Welfare Capitalism" (Week 14), directly addressing social, economic, and political inclusion and inequality reduction (Targets 10.2, 10.3, 10.4).

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: No explicit or inferable evidence related to urban planning, housing, transport, or cultural heritage in cities was found in the course syllabus.

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The "Political Economy" section discusses the "resource curse" (Week 13), which implicitly involves the political challenges of managing natural resources, indirectly relating to Target 12.2.

Climate Action.reason: No explicit or inferable evidence related to climate change, adaptation, mitigation, or climate policy was found in the course syllabus.

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit or inferable evidence related to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable fisheries was found in the course syllabus.

Life on Land.reason: The "resource curse" topic (Week 13) implicitly involves the management of land-based natural resources, offering a very weak, indirect link to this SDG, though not focused on biodiversity.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course, titled "Comparative Politics," fundamentally focuses on the formation, change, and impact of different political institutions, directly addressing effective, accountable, and inclusive governance (Targets 16.6, 16.7).

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The comparative study of political systems and global trends like "Democratic Decline" (Week 8) implicitly relates to policy coherence and global governance, touching upon Targets 17.13 and 17.14.
